debug_info.goal:
R : Type ?u.16996
inst✝ : CommRing R
I J : Ideal R
⊢ ∀ (c : R) {x : R}, x ∈ ↑I ∩ ↑J → c • x ∈ ↑I ∩ ↑J

failed_attempts:
intro c x hx
    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

intro c x hx
    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

intro c x hx
    simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff] at hx ⊢
    have h₁ : c • x ∈ I := by
      -- Since I is an ideal, c • x is in I because x ∈ I and c ∈ R.
      have h₂ : x ∈ I := hx.1
      exact I.smul_mem c h₂
    have h₂ : c • x ∈ J := by
      -- Since J is an ideal, c • x is in J because x ∈ J and c ∈ R.
      have h₃ : x ∈ J := hx.2
      exact J.smul_mem c h₃
    exact ⟨h₁, h₂⟩

intro c x hx
    have hI : x ∈ I := hx.1
    have hJ : x ∈ J := hx.2
    have hI' : c • x ∈ I := I.smul_mem hI
    have hJ' : c • x ∈ J := J.smul_mem hJ
    exact ⟨hI', hJ'⟩

intro c x hx
    constructor
    · -- Prove c • x ∈ I
      exact I.smul_mem c hx.1
    · -- Prove c • x ∈ J
      exact J.smul_mem c hx.2

intro c x hx
    simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff] at hx ⊢
    constructor <;>
    (try simp_all [Ideal.mul_mem_left]) <;>
    (try aesop) <;>
    (try exact I.mul_mem_left c hx.1) <;>
    (try exact J.mul_mem_left c hx.2)

intro c x hx
    -- Since I and J are ideals, they are closed under scalar multiplication.
    -- Given x ∈ I ∩ J, we have x ∈ I and x ∈ J.
    -- Therefore, c • x ∈ I and c • x ∈ J, so c • x ∈ I ∩ J.
    simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff] at hx ⊢
    constructor <;>
      (try simp_all [Ideal.smul_mem]) <;>
      (try aesop)

intro c x hx
    simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff] at hx ⊢
    have h₁ : c • x ∈ I := I.smul_mem _ hx.1
    have h₂ : c • x ∈ J := J.smul_mem _ hx.2
    exact ⟨h₁, h₂⟩

    simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff] at hx ⊢

    have hx₂ : x ∈ J := hx.2

intro c x hx
    have h₁ : x ∈ I := by exact hx.1
    have h₂ : x ∈ J := by exact hx.2
    have h₃ : c • x ∈ I := by
      -- Use the property of I being an ideal to show that c • x ∈ I
      have h₄ : c • x ∈ I := I.smul_mem c h₁
      exact h₄
    have h₄ : c • x ∈ J := by
      -- Similarly, use the property of J being an ideal to show that c • x ∈ J
      have h₅ : c • x ∈ J := J.smul_mem c h₂
      exact h₅
    -- Combine the two results to show that c • x ∈ I ∩ J
    exact ⟨h₃, h₄⟩

intro c x hx
    -- We need to show that c • x is in the intersection of I and J.
    -- Since x ∈ I ∩ J, it is in both I and J. By the absorbing property of ideals, c • x ∈ I and c • x ∈ J.

    have h₁ : x ∈ I := hx.1

    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

    have hI : x ∈ I := by exact hx.1
    have hJ : x ∈ J := by exact hx.2

    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

intro c x hx
    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

intro c x hx
    have hxI : x ∈ I := by
      -- Extract the fact that x is in I from the hypothesis x ∈ I ∩ J
      exact hx.1
    have hxJ : x ∈ J := by
      -- Extract the fact that x is in J from the hypothesis x ∈ I ∩ J
      exact hx.2
    have hI : c • x ∈ I := by
      -- Use the fact that I is an ideal to show that c • x ∈ I
      exact I.smul_mem hxI
    have hJ : c • x ∈ J := by
      -- Use the fact that J is an ideal to show that c • x ∈ J
      exact J.smul_mem hxJ
    -- Combine the results to show that c • x ∈ I ∩ J
    exact ⟨hI, hJ⟩

intro c x hx
    exact ⟨I.smul_mem c hx.1, J.smul_mem c hx.2⟩

intro c x hx
    have hxI : x ∈ (I : Set R) := by
      exact hx.1
    have hcxI : c • x ∈ (I : Set R) := by
      have h₁ : c • x = c * x := by
        simp [smul_eq_mul]
      rw [h₁]
      -- Since I is an ideal, multiplying an element of I by c keeps it in I.
      exact I.mul_mem_left c hxI
    have hxJ : x ∈ (J : Set R) := by
      exact hx.2
    have hcxJ : c • x ∈ (J : Set R) := by
      have h₁ : c • x = c * x := by
        simp [smul_eq_mul]
      rw [h₁]
      -- Since J is an ideal, multiplying an element of J by c keeps it in J.
      exact J.mul_mem_left c hxJ
    have h_main : c • x ∈ (I : Set R) ∩ (J : Set R) := by
      exact ⟨hcxI, hcxJ⟩
    exact h_main

      exact h₄
